title: An updated and conservative systematic review and meta-analysis of epidemiological evidence on psychotic experiences in children and adults: on the pathway from proneness to persistence to dimensional expression across mental disorders.
finding_one_line: Meta-analysis of 61 cohorts: psychotic experiences occur in ~7.2% of the general population (annual incidence 2.5%), far above the schizophrenia diagnosis rate; only 7.4% of people with baseline PE develop a psychotic disorder.

numbers_it_licenses.what_it_is: median prevalence of psychotic experiences in the general population
numbers_it_licenses.abstract_sentence: Estimates of the incidence and prevalence of PE obtained from 61 cohorts revealed a median annual incidence of 2.5% and a prevalence of 7.2%.

numbers_it_licenses.what_it_is: median annual incidence of psychotic experiences
numbers_it_licenses.abstract_sentence: Estimates of the incidence and prevalence of PE obtained from 61 cohorts revealed a median annual incidence of 2.5% and a prevalence of 7.2%.

numbers_it_licenses.what_it_is: proportion with baseline PE who develop a psychotic disorder
numbers_it_licenses.abstract_sentence: Of those with baseline PE, 7.4% develop a psychotic disorder outcome.

title: Psychotic Experiences in the General Population: A Cross-National Analysis Based on 31,261 Respondents From 18 Countries.
finding_one_line: WHO World Mental Health cross-national survey of 31,261 adults across 18 countries: 5.8% report a lifetime psychotic experience; hallucinations far more common than delusions.

numbers_it_licenses.what_it_is: mean lifetime prevalence of any psychotic experience across 18 countries
numbers_it_licenses.abstract_sentence: mean lifetime prevalence (SE) of ever having a PE was 5.8% (0.2%), with hallucinatory experiences (5.2% [0.2%]) much more common than delusional experiences (1.3% [0.1%]).

numbers_it_licenses.what_it_is: lifetime prevalence of hallucinatory experiences
numbers_it_licenses.abstract_sentence: mean lifetime prevalence (SE) of ever having a PE was 5.8% (0.2%), with hallucinatory experiences (5.2% [0.2%]) much more common than delusional experiences (1.3% [0.1%]).

numbers_it_licenses.what_it_is: lifetime prevalence of delusional experiences
numbers_it_licenses.abstract_sentence: mean lifetime prevalence (SE) of ever having a PE was 5.8% (0.2%), with hallucinatory experiences (5.2% [0.2%]) much more common than delusional experiences (1.3% [0.1%]).

title: DSM-5 field trials in the United States and Canada, Part II: test-retest reliability of selected categorical diagnoses.
finding_one_line: DSM-5 field trials framing for how reliably clinicians draw diagnostic lines; schizophrenia's specific kappa is NOT in the abstract, so no schizophrenia-specific number is licensed.

numbers_it_licenses.what_it_is: the 'good' reliability band; nine tested DSM-5 diagnoses fell here
numbers_it_licenses.abstract_sentence: Overall, five diagnoses were in the very good range(kappa=0.60-0.79), nine in the good range(kappa=0.40-0.59), six in the questionable range (kappa = 0.20-0.39), and three in the unacceptable range (kappa values,0.20).

title: Prevalence, correlates, disability, and comorbidity of DSM-IV schizotypal personality disorder: results from the wave 2 national epidemiologic survey on alcohol and related conditions.
journal: Primary Care Companion to the Journal of Clinical Psychiatry
finding_one_line: NESARC survey: schizotypal personality disorder is prevalent (~3.9%), stable, highly disabling trait condition on the schizophrenia spectrum — a persistent personality pattern, not a discrete psychotic episode.

numbers_it_licenses.what_it_is: lifetime prevalence of schizotypal personality disorder in US adults
numbers_it_licenses.abstract_sentence: Lifetime prevalence of SPD was 3.9%, with significantly greater rates among men (4.2%) than women (3.7%) (p < .01).

title: Diagnostic Stability of ICD/DSM First Episode Psychosis Diagnoses: Meta-analysis.
finding_one_line: Meta-analysis of 14,484 first-episode patients: schizophrenia is a highly stable diagnosis (0.90) whereas brief/acute-transient (0.56) and schizophreniform (0.29) are unstable and mostly re-diagnosed as schizophrenia; schizoaffective 0.72.

numbers_it_licenses.what_it_is: prospective diagnostic stability of schizophrenia
numbers_it_licenses.abstract_sentence: Prospective diagnostic stability ranked: schizophrenia 0.90 (95% CI 0.85-0.95) ... schizoaffective disorder 0.72 (95% CI 0.61-0.73) ... acute and transient psychotic disorder/brief psychotic disorder 0.56 ... schizophreniform disorder 0.29 (95% CI 0.22-0.38).

numbers_it_licenses.what_it_is: diagnostic stability of schizophreniform disorder (lowest)
numbers_it_licenses.abstract_sentence: schizophreniform disorder 0.29 (95% CI 0.22-0.38).

title: Rates and Predictors of Conversion to Schizophrenia or Bipolar Disorder Following Substance-Induced Psychosis.
finding_one_line: Danish registry cohort (N=6,788): 32.2% of substance-induced psychoses convert to schizophrenia or bipolar, rising to 47.4% for cannabis-induced psychosis; median ~3.1 years to schizophrenia.

numbers_it_licenses.what_it_is: overall conversion rate from substance-induced psychosis
numbers_it_licenses.abstract_sentence: Overall, 32.2% (95% CI=29.7-34.9) of patients with a substance-induced psychosis converted to either bipolar or schizophrenia-spectrum disorders.

numbers_it_licenses.what_it_is: conversion rate for cannabis-induced psychosis (highest)
numbers_it_licenses.abstract_sentence: The highest conversion rate was found for cannabis-induced psychosis, with 47.4% (95% CI=42.7-52.3) converting to either schizophrenia or bipolar disorder.

title: Substance-induced psychoses converting into schizophrenia: a register-based study of 18,478 Finnish inpatient cases.
finding_one_line: Finnish register cohort (N=18,478): 8-year cumulative conversion to schizophrenia-spectrum was 46% after cannabis-induced psychosis, 30% after amphetamine-induced, 5.0% after alcohol-induced.

numbers_it_licenses.what_it_is: 8-year cumulative risk of schizophrenia-spectrum after cannabis-induced psychosis
numbers_it_licenses.abstract_sentence: Eight-year cumulative risk to receive a schizophrenia spectrum diagnosis was 46% (95% CI, 35%-57%) for persons with a diagnosis of cannabis-induced psychosis and 30% (95% CI, 14%-46%) for those with an amphetamine-induced psychosis.

numbers_it_licenses.what_it_is: 8-year cumulative risk after alcohol-induced psychosis
numbers_it_licenses.abstract_sentence: 8-year cumulative risk for subsequent schizophrenia spectrum diagnosis was only 5.0% (95% CI, 4.6%-5.5%).

title: Diagnostic shift in patients diagnosed with schizoaffective disorder: a systematic review and meta-analysis of rediagnosis studies.
finding_one_line: Meta-analysis of 31 rediagnosis studies: schizoaffective disorder is diagnostically unstable — 36% later re-diagnosed (19% to schizophrenia, 14% to affective), underscoring mood burden as the shifting discriminator.

numbers_it_licenses.what_it_is: proportion of schizoaffective patients whose diagnosis later shifts
numbers_it_licenses.abstract_sentence: A total of 36% of patients with a diagnosis of SAD at first assessment switch, many to schizophrenia (19%), 14% to affective disorders, and 6% to other disorders.

title: A systematic review of the incidence of schizophrenia: the distribution of rates and the influence of sex, urbanicity, migrant status and methodology.
finding_one_line: Systematic review (1,458 rates, 33 countries): median schizophrenia incidence 15.2 per 100,000, male excess (M/F 1.40), migrant excess (4.6).

numbers_it_licenses.what_it_is: median incidence of schizophrenia
numbers_it_licenses.abstract_sentence: the distribution of rates was asymmetric and had a median value (10%-90% quantile) of 15.2 (7.7-43.0) per 100,000.

numbers_it_licenses.what_it_is: male/female incidence rate ratio
numbers_it_licenses.abstract_sentence: the male/female rate ratio median (10%-90% quantile) was 1.40 (0.9-2.4).

title: A systematic review of the prevalence of schizophrenia.
finding_one_line: Systematic review of 188 studies from 46 countries: lifetime prevalence ~4.0 per 1,000, lifetime morbid risk ~7.2 per 1,000 (well under 1%).

numbers_it_licenses.what_it_is: median lifetime prevalence of schizophrenia
numbers_it_licenses.abstract_sentence: The median values per 1,000 persons (10%-90% quantiles) for ... lifetime, and lifetime morbid risk were ... 4.0 (1.6-12.1), and 7.2 (3.1-27.1), respectively.

numbers_it_licenses.what_it_is: median lifetime morbid risk (~0.7%)
numbers_it_licenses.abstract_sentence: The median values per 1,000 persons ... lifetime morbid risk were ... 7.2 (3.1-27.1), respectively.

title: Gender difference in age at onset of schizophrenia: a meta-analysis.
finding_one_line: Meta-analysis (48,620 patients): men develop schizophrenia only ~1.07 years earlier than women — a real but smaller sex gap than classic teaching, and absent in ICD-based studies.

numbers_it_licenses.what_it_is: pooled male-earlier gender difference in age at first admission
numbers_it_licenses.abstract_sentence: A random-effects model gave a pooled estimate of the gender difference of 1.07 years (95% confidence interval 0.21-1.93) for age at first admission of schizophrenia, with males having earlier onset.

title: Age at onset of mental disorders worldwide: large-scale meta-analysis of 192 epidemiological studies.
finding_one_line: Meta-analysis of 192 studies (n=708,561): schizophrenia-spectrum onset peaks at 20.5 years (median 25), ~48% before age 25, and no significant sex difference.

numbers_it_licenses.what_it_is: peak age at onset for schizophrenia-spectrum disorders
numbers_it_licenses.abstract_sentence: schizophrenia-spectrum disorders/primary psychotic states: 3%, 12.3%, 47.8%, 20.5 years (k = 36, median = 25, IQR = 20-34)

numbers_it_licenses.what_it_is: age at onset did not differ significantly by sex
numbers_it_licenses.abstract_sentence: No significant difference emerged by sex, or definition of age of onset.

title: Paul Eugen Bleuler and the origin of the term schizophrenia (SCHIZOPRENIEGRUPPE).
finding_one_line: Historical/terminological source: Eugen Bleuler coined 'schizophrenia' (a splitting among psychic functions, not a split of personality); Europe PMC record carries NO abstractText so licenses no number.
